title: Puppy Chewing Solutions (2026) – How to Stop Destructive Chewing
excerpt: Practical solutions for puppy chewing and teething destruction. Age-appropriate redirects, safe chew toys, and management strategies that work.
quickAnswer: Puppy chewing is normal and peaks during teething at 4–6 months. Redirect to appropriate chew toys, puppy-proof your space, and never punish after the fact. Frozen rubber toys and bully sticks satisfy the chewing urge safely.
whoThisIsFor: Puppy parents dealing with furniture destruction
Anyone whose puppy chews shoes, cords, or baseboards

sections.heading: Why Puppies Chew Everything
sections.content:
Chewing is as natural to puppies as crawling is to babies. Understanding the **why** helps you choose the right solution.

**Teething (3–7 months)** — Baby teeth fall out, adult teeth push through, and gums are sore and itchy. Chewing provides relief. This is the most intense chewing phase and it's completely involuntary — your puppy isn't being "bad," they're in pain.

**Exploration** — Puppies explore the world with their mouths. They don't have hands, so tasting and chewing is how they learn about textures, materials, and objects.

**Boredom** — An under-stimulated puppy will find entertainment. If you haven't provided appropriate outlets, your couch legs become the next best thing.

**Anxiety** — Some puppies chew when stressed, particularly during alone time. If destruction only happens when you're away, separation anxiety may be the root cause. See our [separation anxiety guide](/guides/separation-anxiety-and-barking).

The solution is never punishment. Yelling at a puppy for chewing something 10 minutes ago teaches them nothing except that you're unpredictable. Instead, manage the environment and redirect to appropriate outlets.

sections.heading: Best Chew Toys by Age
sections.content:
Not all chew toys are created equal, and the wrong toy at the wrong age can be dangerous.

**8–12 weeks (baby teeth)**:
- Soft rubber toys (puppy-specific Kongs)
- Rope toys for gentle tugging
- Frozen wet washcloths for teething relief
- Avoid: hard nylon bones, antlers, ice cubes

**3–6 months (active teething)**:
- Frozen stuffed Kongs (peanut butter + kibble)
- Rubber teething rings
- Bully sticks (6-inch, supervised)
- Frozen carrots (natural, low-calorie)
- Avoid: cooked bones, rawhide, toys small enough to swallow

**6–12 months (adult teeth emerging)**:
- Graduated to medium-hard rubber toys
- Himalayan yak chews
- Antlers (split, not whole) — supervised only
- Stuffable puzzle toys
- Avoid: tennis balls (abrasive coating wears teeth), sticks

**The rotation strategy**: keep 3–4 chew toys available and rotate weekly. "New" toys are always more interesting than familiar ones. This simple trick reduces boredom chewing by up to 60%.

Browse age-appropriate [puppy supplies](/collections/puppy-essentials) and [training accessories](/collections/dog-training-accessories).

sections.heading: Puppy-Proofing Strategies That Work
sections.content:
Management prevents problems. Every pair of shoes your puppy destroys is a management failure, not a training failure.

**Level 1: Remove targets**
- Shoes in closets, doors closed
- Charging cables in cord covers or behind furniture
- Remote controls in drawers
- Children's toys off the floor
- Houseplants on high shelves (many are toxic)

**Level 2: Block access**
- Baby gates to restrict rooms
- Exercise pens for safe confinement
- Crate when unsupervised
- Bitter apple spray on furniture legs and baseboards

**Level 3: Supervise actively**
- Umbilical training — clip leash to your belt so puppy stays within arm's reach
- Redirect the instant you see interest in a forbidden object
- "Trade" forbidden items for treats (never chase the puppy)

**The bitter apple spray approach**: works for about 60% of puppies. Apply to furniture legs, door frames, and baseboards. Reapply every 2–3 days. Some puppies actually like the taste — test on a cotton ball first.

For puppies who chew during alone time, ensure they have a [stuffed Kong and safe chew toys](/collections/puppy-essentials) available in their crate or pen.

sections.heading: When Chewing Signals a Bigger Problem
sections.content:
Most puppy chewing is normal. But some patterns indicate issues that need professional attention.

**Separation anxiety chewing**:
- Destruction only happens when you're away
- Targets door frames, window sills, crate bars
- Accompanied by excessive drooling, panting, pacing
- Your puppy follows you room to room

**Pica (eating non-food items)**:
- Regularly swallowing rocks, fabric, plastic
- This is a medical and behavioral emergency
- Can cause life-threatening intestinal blockages
- Consult your veterinarian immediately

**Compulsive chewing**:
- Chewing their own paws, tail, or flank excessively
- May indicate allergies, pain, or anxiety disorder
- Veterinary evaluation recommended

**When to call a trainer**:
- Destructive chewing hasn't decreased by 8 months
- Your puppy becomes aggressive when you try to redirect
- Chewing escalates despite appropriate management

A certified positive-reinforcement trainer (look for CPDT-KA credentials) can assess your specific situation and create a customized behavior modification plan. See our [training tools guide](/guides/dog-training-tools-explained) for more on professional-grade supplies.

faq.question: At what age do puppies stop chewing everything?
faq.answer: Most puppies outgrow intense chewing by 12–18 months as teething ends and impulse control develops. Some breeds (especially retrievers and working dogs) maintain a strong chewing drive into adulthood and benefit from lifelong chew toy access.

faq.question: Is it okay to give my puppy ice cubes for teething?
faq.answer: Small ice chips are generally fine, but large ice cubes can chip puppy teeth. Better options include frozen wet washcloths, frozen stuffed Kongs, and frozen carrots, which provide teething relief without the tooth-damage risk.

faq.question: Should I punish my puppy for chewing my shoes?
faq.answer: Never. Punishment after the fact doesn't work — your puppy can't connect punishment to something they chewed 10 minutes ago. Instead, manage the environment by keeping shoes out of reach and redirect to appropriate chew toys.

faq.question: How do I stop my puppy from chewing furniture?
faq.answer: Apply bitter apple spray to furniture legs, provide appropriate chew toy alternatives within reach, supervise actively, and use baby gates or a crate when you can't watch them. Frozen Kongs redirect the chewing urge effectively.
